subject: Surgery
title: Transplantation
preambleMarkdown:
# Transplantation

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Transplantation is a procedure or practical clinical workflow in surgery.
- This article focuses on indications, anatomy, perioperative risk, operative options, postoperative care, and complications.
- The practical clinical priority is safe performance, correct indication, and complication prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Transplantation to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Transplantation is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is safe performance, correct indication, and complication prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnostic, therapeutic, emergent, urgent, or elective use.
- Bedside, image-guided, endoscopic, operative, or hybrid approach.
- Low-risk versus high-risk patient based on bleeding, infection, airway, anatomy, anticoagulation, and comorbid disease.
- Initial procedure, repeat procedure, failed procedure, or complication-management setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Transplantation depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In surgery, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Transplantation.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Transplantation disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Transplantation should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Transplantation first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Transplantation, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The clinical presentation is the problem that makes the procedure necessary.
- Assess severity, stability, anatomy, bleeding risk, infection risk, allergies, medications, and prior procedures.
- The most important history is what changes indication, urgency, consent, sedation, or aftercare.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Transplantation, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused surgery examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Transplantation presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Transplantation with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Transplantation is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Transplantation should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm indication, alternatives, risks, consent, equipment, positioning, monitoring, and post-procedure plan.
- Correct modifiable risks when time allows, especially anticoagulation, coagulopathy, infection, and unstable physiology.
- Stop or escalate if anatomy is unclear, complications develop, or the intended endpoint is not reached.

### Initial Management

- Confirm the indication, urgency, consent pathway, patient identity, site, equipment, and monitoring plan for Transplantation.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses safe performance, correct indication, and complication prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Perform the procedure only when the indication, anatomy, equipment, operator skill, and rescue plan are acceptable.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Transplantation, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Transplantation may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Transplantation patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Transplantation, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Transplantation in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Transplantation, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Transplantation does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Transplantation may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Transplantation, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Transplantation as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the indication, contraindication, key anatomy, major complication, and aftercare.
- Document neurovascular status, bleeding risk, consent, and response when relevant.
- Never let the desire to complete a procedure override patient stability or unsafe anatomy.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Transplantation draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: Imported Source Notes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
<!-- source: step-up-surgery import start -->
Source: Step-Up to Surgery, 2E. Imported on 2026-07-14.

### Source Excerpt: Organ Supply and Demand

_Source lines: 27092-27282._

```text
ORGAN SUPPLY AND DEMAND
I. Organ Allocation
A. There is a large discrepancy between the demand (number of patients awaiting
transplantation) and the supply (number of actual organ donors and transplant
able organs) . For deceased donor organs , there is also a discrepancy between
the location of the donor and the potential recipient who , according to current
algorithms, might best use these transplantable organs .

B. The national Organ Procurement and Transplant Network (OPTN) was
established in 1 984 through the National Organ Transplant Act, passed by the
Congress of the United States. The intent of the act is to ensure that scarce
transplantable organs are distributed equitably, safely, and eciently.

C. The federal contract to administer the OPTN has been awarded to the United Network
for Organ Sharing (UNOS) , which has its headquarters in Richmond, Virginia.

l . All organ transplant centers , organ procurement organizations (OPOs) , and
tissue typing laboratories must meet and maintain standards set by UNOS to
maintain membership in the network and access to deceased donor organs .

2. UNOS maintains the national "waiting list" of patients awaiting a transplant.

a. Each time a deceased donor organ becomes available for transplantation
anywhere in the nation, this computerized database is searched, and trans
plant candidates are ranked according to predetermined parameters.

b. It is important to note that the "list" is not merely a static ordinal rank of
waiting time. The results of a match run are determined by multiple factors,
of which waiting time is only one factor and not the most important factor
for any organ.

D. The Scientific Registry of Transplant Recipients (SRTR) is responsible for main
taining a very extensive database on all listed patients awaiting transplantation
and follow-up on all transplanted patients . SRTR data are easily accessible at
http ://www.ustransplant. org, and this website is a useful source of information
for patients and health care providers.

E. Another useful resource is the United States Renal Data System (USRDS)
(http ://usrds. org) , which maintains an extensive database on all patients who
have enrolled in the End-Stage Renal Disease Program .

F. There is a different allocation algorithm for different deceased donor organs .

These algorithms take in consideration multiple factors related to the recipient,
donor, and logistics.

l. Recipient factors include severity of illness for liver transplantation, loss of
access for dialysis for renal transplantation, age (pediatric recipients receive
a slight increased preference) , and the likelihood of receiving a transplant
(e.g. , panel reactive antibody [ PRA] for patients awaiting renal transplants) .

2. Donor factors include donor age , past medical history, social history, donor
stability, and biopsy results of donor organs .

## 3 . Logistics primarily considers the distance (time of transport) between donor

and recipient hospitals.

G. OPOs identify potential deceased organ donors. The 57 nonprofit OPOs provide
service to all 50 states and Puerto Rico.

l. OPOs facilitate and coordinate organ recovery, preservation, and transport.

Law requires that all deaths are reported to the OPO.

## 2 . OPOs are also charged with education of health care providers and the lay

public regarding organ donation.

II. Donor Organs from Diseased Individuals
A. Organ donation: Organs for transplantation may be recovered from individuals who
meet the criteria for brain death or from individuals who have massive and irrevers
ible brain damage and for whom an independent decision to withdraw life support
has been made (nonheart-beating donor [ NHBD ] , or donation after cardiac death) .

l . The basic legal Xamework for deHning brain death is described in the Uniform
Determination of Death Act ( 1 980) , which has been adopted by all states.

T R A N S P L A N T AT I O N e 365
In this act, brain death is defined as the "irreversible cessation of all functions
of the entire brain, including the brainstem. "

## 2 . Detailed specifics for clinical brain death (e.g. , length of time for apnea) , and

requirements for confirmatory testing (e.g. , electroencephalogram, cerebral
blood flow studies) are the prerogative of individual hospitals .

## 3 . The standards to determine brain death are rigorous to exclude any conceivable

potentially reversible factors (e.g. , hypothermia, alcohol, drugs , severe meta
bolic abnormalities) .

4. The criteria for brain death include:
a. A known and irreversible brain injury
b. Coma on a ventilator
c. Absent brain stem reflexes (e.g. , pupillary, corneal, gag, vestibuloocular,
oculocephalic)
d. Apnea off a ventilator
e. Absence of exclusionary criteria
S. Some hospitals require conHrmatory tests (brain blood flow tests, electroenceph
alogram) , although these have not been listed as mandatory in formal criteria.

6. NHBDs are comatose individuals with severe, irreversible damage , who do not
meet all the formal criteria (usually, some remnant of brain stem activity) for
brain death and for whom an independent decision has been made to withdraw
life support. Organ recovery is performed after there is cessation of cardiac
function in the donor.

## 7 . Recoverable solid organs for transplantation include the heart, lung, liver,

pancreas (whole organ, or islets of Langerhans) , kidney, and intestine. Donors
may also donate tissue, including bone, tendons , heart valves, and corneas.

8. The criteria for what is considered an acceptable organ for transplant have
expanded as the number of people awaiting transplant has increased. The
clinical need of an individual patient is taken into account when deciding on
the suitability of a given organ offer.

9. Contraindications to organ donation include malignancy, HIV, systemic infec
tion, hepatitis B surface antigen positivity, hepatitis C antibody (many centers
use organs from hepatitis C + donors for hepatitis C + recipients) .

B. Organ preservation: Because the tolerance of transplantable organs to normothermic
ischemia is measured in minutes, effective steps must be taken to protect against
cell damage and cell death.

l . Normothermic cellular metabolism in the absence of oxygen and the metabolic
substrates supplied by an intact circulation leads to cell damage and death.

The products of anaerobic cellular metabolism play a major role in reperfusion
injury (e.g. , oxygen-free radicals) upon the reestablishment of normothermic
circulation to the transplanted organ.

2. The strategy of organ preservation is to minimize cellular metabolism.

3. Hypothermia (less than SC) is a cornerstone of all organ preservation.

Oxygen consumption is reduced by 9S% at S0C. Freezing, with intracellular
ice-crystal formation, must be avoided.

a. Hypothermia does not totally stop metabolic processes, and the end
products of metabolism accumulate over time. This is a major factor in the
tolerable length of time between organ recovery and transplantation.

b . Energy-independent cell functions are less affected by hypothermia (e.g. ,
passive diffusion of extracellular sodium across thecal membrane) . Organ
preservation solutions approximate the intracellular environment with a
high potassium and low sodium concentrations.

c. Cellular swelling is a natural consequence of hypothermic storage due
to passive diffusion of water into the higher oncotic pressure of the cell.

Impermeable oncotic agents (e.g. , hetastarch, mannitol, raffinose) are
included to prevent cell swelling.

4. Accumulation of lactic acid from low-level anaerobic metabolism leads to
a progressively lower pH within the stored organ. Phosphates are the usual
buffer for organ preservation solutions.

## 5 . Several commercial organ preservation solutions with different components

and concentrations have been approved for clinical use, including Collins ,
ViaS pan (University of Wisconsin Solution) , and Custodial (HTK) .

6. Optimal organ preservation begins with careful clinical management of the
deceased donor, with special attention to hemodynamic and metabolic param
eters. Hypotension is common, and many donors have diabetes insipidus and
marked hypernatremia. Severe hypernatremia in the donor has been associated
with allograft dysfunction (especially liver allografts) .

C. Organ recovery
l . The operative approach is directed toward minimizing warm ischemia time as
well as surgical injury to the recovered organs .

2. Proximal and distal control of the aorta for cold perfusion is carried out early
in the operation. Access to the vena cava is required to vent blood and flush
solution.

## 3 . In a stable donor, time may be expended to do a preliminary dissection, mobi

lizing individual organs and looking for anatomic variants (e.g. , replaced right
and/or left hepatic arteries ) . However, many transplant programs prefer to use
a rapid technique of en bloc excision after cold perfusion with dissection and
separation on the back table.
```

### Source Excerpt: Transplantation of Specific Organs

_Source lines: 27283-27746._

```text
TRANSPLANTATION Of SPECIFIC ORGANS
I. Kidney
A. Indications
l . End-stage renal disease with a glomerular filtration rate (GFR) less than
20 mUmin-1 or maintenance hemodialysis are prerequisites for a deceased
donor transplant.

2. Preemptive renal transplant (GFR greater than 20 mUmin, or patient not yet
on dialysis) may be performed using living donors.

3. Conditions that are some of the more common causes of renal failure leading
to renal transplantation, such as diabetic nephropathy, hypertensive nephropa
thy, glomerulonephritides, polycystic kidney disease, chronic pyelonephritis,
obstructive uropathy, or congenital abnormalities
B. Contraindications
l . Chronic, untreated infection: HIV, hepatitis B, and hepatitis C are no longer
absolute contraindications to transplantation, but they must be approached
with a meticulous plan for long-term management.

2. A recent malignancy (except small nonmelanoma skin cancer and, at some
centers, carcinoma in situ) or metastatic cancer: A 2- to 5-year disease-free
interval following curative treatment of a malignant tumor is required at most
centers.

3. Severe comorbid conditions that make the risk of surgery and/or chronic
immunosuppression prohibitive.

4. A history of recurrent noncompliance is a warning sign for inability to comply
with the complicated postoperative management requirements .

C. Preoperative evaluation
l . A complete history and physical examination may identify many signiHcant,
but correctable, potential problems for transplantation. Evaluation of the lower
urinary tract is often indicated based on history (e.g. , recurrent infections,
diabetes mellitus [ neurogenic bladder] ) . Anuric patients have no voiding
symptoms.

2. Bilateral native nephrectomy is indicated for chronic infection, severe
proteinuria, suspicion of cancer in the native kidneys , or massively enlarged
kidneys Xom polycystic kidney disease with pain, infection, or bleeding.

Bilateral native nephrectomy for refractory hypertension has been reported
to be helpful in some cases .

## 3 . The patient has HLA typing and screening for preformed anti-HLA antibodies

(PRAs) prior to being placed on the UNOS waiting list.

T R A N S P L A N T AT I O N e 367
D . Operation
l . The kidney is transplanted into the heterotopic location of the iliac fossa. The
renal artery and vein are anastomosed to the respective iliac artery and vein.

2. Following reperfusion, the ureter is anastomosed to the urinary bladder. An
antireflux maneuver (Litch technique [ currently favored] or Politano-Lead
[better] technique) is performed. The ipsilateral native ureter may be used.

E . Immediate postoperative care
l . Most aspects of management are the same as they would be for any general
surgical patient. Attention to fluid and electrolyte management is particularly
important. Hypovolemia (which can occur rapidly with a brisk diuresis) must
be avoided.

2. Special attention must be paid to frequent monitoring of urine output.

Approximately 20% of deceased donor renal transplants have delayed graft
function with anuria or oliguria.

a. Because serious and graft-threatening technical complications (e.g. , thrombosis,
urine leak) may present with low urine output, this must be a diagnosis of ex
clusion, and causes of low urine output must be diligently sought and treated.

b. Causes of low urine output include hypovolemia and hypotension, an oc
cluded Foley catheter, arterial or venous thrombosis , hydronephrosis or
urine leak, and antibody-mediated rej ection.

F. Surgical complications
l . Urine leak may occur in as many as 1 0% of cases and is most Xequently
caused by the poor blood supply to the distal transplant ureter. Urine leak may
be diagnosed by biochemical analysis of fluid from the wound or nuclear scan.

a. Urine leaks in the very early post-transplant period should be treated by
surgical revision of the anastomosis.

b. Urine leaks in the later post-transplant period should be evaluated with cys
toscopy/retrograde or percutaneous nephrostogram because such leaks are
frequently associated with ureteral stenosis.

2. Lymphoceles are collections of lymph from divided lymphatic vessels along the iliac
vessels. Lymphoceles have been reported to occur in as many as 20% of patients.

a. Many of these collections are small and have no clinical impact. These re
quire no intervention.

b. Large lymphoceles may cause allograft dysfunction (by compression or hydro
nephrosis) , pain, or deep venous thrombosis (compression of iliac vein) . Percu
taneous drainage, sometimes combined with sclerotherapy of the cavity, may be
adequate treatment. Lymphoceles may recur, and they are treated with internal
drainage between lymphocele cavity and the peritoneal cavity (fenestration) .

Fenestration may be accomplished by either open or laparoscopic approach.

G. Recurrent disease: Many of the diseases that cause end-stage renal disease in the
native kidneys may recur in the transplanted kidney (Table 20-3 ) . Recurrent
disease does not always lead to allograft loss from recurrent disease.

Oxa l o s i s
Mem branoprol iferative g l omerulonephritis I M P G N ) type 2
Hemolytic uremic syndrome I H U S )

Foca l segmental g l omeru l osclerosis IFSGS)

TAB LE 20-4 dney Donor Status and Survival
Status of Donor
Living donor kidney tra n s p l a nt
Deceased donor kidney transpla nt
H. Long-term outcomes

## 1 -Year Survival

95%
90%
5-Year Survival
80%
65%
l. Current graft survival (dialysis independence) is shown in Table 20-4.

## 2 . There are no randomized prospective trials comparing chronic dialysis to

transplantation.

## 3 . There are several "intent to treat" studies comparing outcomes of patients on

dialysis awaiting transplant and those actually transplanted. After the increased
mortality associated with the operation itself, and early higher dose immuno
suppression, the survival of transplanted patients is superior to dialysis after
8 months.

II. Pancreas
A. Indications
l . The majority of pancreas transplants are performed in patients with type I dia
betes mellitus. Diabetes mellitus auects nearly 14 million Americans , and it is
the sixth leading cause of death (National Center for Health Statistics) . It is a
leading cause of blindness and renal failure.

## 2 . Pancreas transplantation is most frequently performed in combination with

a kidney transplant (simultaneous pancreas-kidney [ SPK] transplant) . The
pancreas may also be transplanted into nonrenal failure diabetics (pancreas
transplant alone [ PTA] ) , or into diabetics with a previous renal transplant
(pancreas after kidney [ PAK] transplant) .

B. Contraindications
l . Contraindications for pancreas transplantation are essentially the same as
those for kidney transplantation.

## 2 . Because the incidence of atherosclerotic disease is so high in patients with

diabetes, especially in patients with renal failure , an aggressive investigation of
coronary and peripheral vascular disease should be performed.

C. Operation
l . The pancreas is transplanted with the adjacent duodenum, with which it
shares a blood supply. The spleen is separated from the tail of the pancreas
prior to transplantation.

2 . There have been multiple techniques of implantation of the pancreas graft.

The two main considerations are the location of the vascular anastomoses and
the technique of managing exocrine drainage.

3. The venous (portal) drainage is still most frequently into the systemic
circulation (iliac vein or inferior vena cava [ IVC J ) . Several centers advocate
drainage directly into the portal venous system (superior mesenteric vein or
splenic vein) . There is good clinical evidence that portal drainage is associated
with a lower incidence of rej ection.

4. Enteric drainage may be either into small bowel (Roux-en-Y or side-to-side
anastomosis) or into the urinary bladder.

5. Bladder drainage is associated with metabolic acidosis , due to obligatory
loss of the high pH pancreatic secretions , dehydration, "reflux pancreatitis , "
cystitis , and bleeding. Monitoring of urinary amylase excretion may aid
in the diagnosis of rej ection, and the pancreas graft can be biopsied
cystoscopically.

6. A functioning pancreas allograft rapidly establishes euglycemia , and "rebound
hypoglycemia" is rare.

T R A N S P L A N T AT I O N e 369
D. Long-term outcomes
l. The potential of normalized glucose control (as reflected by a normal hemo
globin [ Hgb ] A 1 C) to prevent the severity and progression of the complica
tions of diabetes has been well demonstrated by the Diabetes Control and
Complications Trial (DCCT) (see New England journal of Medicine. 1 993 ;
329 [ 14 ] ) .

## 2 . Successful pancreas transplantation can establish euglycemia and a normal

ized Hgb A 1 C . Current data suggest that successful pancreas transplantation
can improve peripheral neuropathy and stabilize or even reduce diabetic
retinopathy. There is no evidence that successful pancreas transplantation
reverses macrovascular disease.

E. Islet transplantation
l . The whole pancreas transplanted to correct diabetes mellitus contains less than
2% of its mass, the insulin-producing islets of Langerhans . Experimental stud
ies demonstrating that isolated islet transplantation can correct diabetes were
published by Lacy and Ballinger more than 30 years ago . Clinical attempts
to use islet transplantation had low success rates of marginal durability. The
Edmonton Protocol (New England ]ournaff of Medicine. 2000 ;343 [ 3 ] ) is a new
immunosuppression protocol that uses daclizumab , tacrolimus, and sirolimus
and is steroid-Xee. Seven consecutive patients were insulin independent, with
median follow-up of 1 year.

2. Islets of Langerhans can be isolated from the pancreas using a combination
of mechanical and enzymatic techniqu es. The isolated islets are inj ected
into a branch of the portal vein in the liver. Islets from two to three donor
pancreases are usually needed to achieve sufficient islet mass to correct
diabetes.

Ill. Liver
A. General principles
l . Indications for liver transplantation include acute hepatic failure and chronic
liver failure.

## 2 . Before the advent of surgery, the 1 -year mortality for patients with decom

pensated liver failure reached more than 50%. Since transplantation has been
available, the survival reaches 85% at 1 year and 70% at 5 years.

B. Transplantation for acute liver failure
l . Acute liver failure is defined as acute decompensation of hepatic function,
with the time from onset of jaundice to encephalopathy in less than 8 weeks.

2. The most common reasons for fulminant liver failure in the United States
are acetaminophen (Tylenol) overdose (20%) , followed by non-A or non-E
hepatitis (also called cryptogenic liver failure) ( 1 5%) , and drugs ( 1 2%) .

## 3 . Presentation and accurate diagnosis begins with a thorough history and physical

examination.

a. Most patients are encephalopathic on presentation, and history can be
obtained from the family.

b. Identify findings of depression and medication overdose.

c. Additionally, patients may have anorexia and malaise.

d. Symptoms are suggestive of a viral syndrome.

4. Prompt treatment includes admission to the intensive care unit with appropriate
resuscitation.

a. Follow liver function tests and chemistries closely.

b. Initiate lactulose therapy early when patient is encephalopathic.

c. Treat acetaminophen overdose with N-acetyl cysteine.

d. Early referral to transplant center is critical for successful outcomes.

## 5 . King's College and Clichy criteria identify the degree of disease and the

appropriate candidates that would beneHt from transplantation in a patient
with acute hepatic failure.

6. Research interest: Some centers use artificial liver support systems to bridge a
patient to liver transplantation. These are temporary measures.

p l a ntati o n . W ith k i d n e y and

tra n s p l antati o n . b 
C. Transplantation for chronic liver failure
l . In the United States, common reasons for transplantation in a patient with
chronic liver failure include alcohol-induced cirrhosis and the myriad of
viral hepatitides. Other causes of liver failure include primary sclerosing
cholangitis , primary biliary cirrhosis , hepatocellular carcinoma, biliary atresia,
metabolic disorders such as hemochromatosis , Wilson disease, and enzymatic
deficiencies in the urea cycle.

## 2 . The pathophysiology of chronic liver failure involves two basic concepts: por

tal hypertension and hepatocyte damage. The increased portal venous blood
flow through the liver results in blood flow via the physiologic portosystemic
shunts as a compensatory mechanism to increased portal blood flow. Hepato
cyte damage results from insult at a cellular level by the virus or other toxins .

## 3 . Selection for liver transplantation involves identification of contraindications

(HIV, advanced age, disseminated cancer) , degree of liver disease, availability
of psychosocial support group (to assist in postoperative care) , and financial
ability (postoperative care requires strict adherence to immunosuppressive
regimens that may be expensive) .

D . Surgical techniques
l . Surgery can be especially diuicult in a patient who is coagulopathic and has
evidence of portal hypertension.

2 . The native liver is mobilized by releasing the attachments from the diaphragm.

The portal triad, which includes the bile duct, portal vein, and the hepatic
artery, are skeletonized in preparation of the donor organ. The suprahepatic
IVC is mobilized. Following this, these structures are clamped and transected,
releasing the liver from the bed.

3. The donor organ is prepared in a similar way, followed by end-to-end anas
tomosis of the suprahepatic and infrahepatic IV C. The portal vein, hepatic
artery, and finally the bile duct anastomosis are completed in that order.

4. Occasionally, a split liver transplant may be performed in a child, wherein an
adult liver cannot be accommodated into the abdomen.

E. Postoperative complications
l . Bleeding: usually occurs when coagulopathy is not adequately addressed
2. Primary nonfunction of the liver: This disastrous situation occurs when the
liver is unable to function after transplantation. This condition warrants
urgent retransplantation.

## 3 . Reperfusion injury: This scenario is common when the ischemic time on the

organ is prolonged. The treatment is primarily supportive.

4. Vascular complications: Following the hepatic arterial anastomosis, the artery
may thrombose, leading to elevations of the transaminases, abscess formation,
and biliary tract strictures . This usually results from a technical error.

a. Acute portal hypertension after transplant should alert the physician to this
complication.

b . Treatment may be angiography with lytic therapy and stenting. Rarely
portal vein thrombosis may occur.

5. Biliary tract stricture: Strictures in the tract occur primarily due to ischemia of
the tract. These can be treated with endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancrea
tography and stent placement.

6. Rej ection: Early rej ection can occur if immunosuppressive therapy is not
initiated early. The patient may be asymptomatic or may have a rise in the
transaminases and bilirubin.

a. The presence of inflammatory cells and lymphocyte-mediated bile duct
injury as seen in the biopsy can be diagnostic. The diagnosis is conHrmed
by biopsy.

b. This condition warrants the administration of immunoglobulin or high
dose steroids.

7. Infection: Most commonly, CMV infection may result. One must always main
tain a very low threshold for infection in a post-transplant patient. Workup
includes blood, urine, and stool cultures for bacteria, fungi, and viruses .

T R A N S P L A N T AT I O N e 3 7 1

## 8 . Recurrence o f native disease: Patients who are transplanted for hepatitis B and

hepatitis C are prone to infecting the donor organ. Primary sclerosing cholan
gitis , biliary cirrhosis , hemochromatosis , and autoimmune hepatitis may occur
in the donor organ.

## 9 . PTLD : As mentioned earlier in the chapter, post-transplant patients may be

susceptible to this malignancy.

F. Outcome: Since the advent of transplantation, approximately 1 0 ,000 liver trans
plants have been performed. This population has a 1 -year patient survival rate of
90% , and a 5-year patient survival rate of 75%. More importantly, these patients
have a dramatic improvement in their quality of life.

IV. Small Bowel
A. The importance of bowel transplantation has been recognized for decades , and
yet the first successful transplant was not performed until l988, in Germany.

Very few centers in the United States currently undertake the daunting task of
intestinal transplant.

B. Indications for this operation involve cases of short gut (less than 50 em) ,
intestinal disorders , or malabsorption syndromes . These include Crohn disease,
trauma with multiple bowel resections , necrotizing enterocolitis, gastroschisis,
and intestinal atresia . It is important to understand that there is no specific
disease entity that warrants intestinal transplant.

C. The procedure involves the removal of the donor bowel, maintaining the superior
mesenteric artery (SMA) and celiac axis . The transplant is conducted by anasto
mosis of the SMA to the aorta, with venous drainage into the IVC or the portal
vein. The maximal ischemia time for bowel is about 6 hours .

D . Complications are failure of graft, infection, and rej ection.
```

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.
